topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I watched Liam laugh, a bright, easy sound that used to be reserved solely for me. Now, it was directed at someone else, a stranger whose presence seemed to fill the space where my history used to reside. My jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath my skin. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that felt like cracking glass. "That's great," I managed, the words sounding flat and devoid of any warmth. I shifted my weight, the cheap plastic chair scraping against the linoleum floor. Every shared memory we’d built—the whispered secrets under the bleachers, the inside jokes that no one else could decipher—felt suddenly like a fragile, discarded costume. When he finally turned to me, his eyes held a polite blankness, a vast, empty expanse where recognition should have been. I didn't flinch, but a cold, sharp feeling settled deep in my chest, like ice water spreading through my core. I stood up abruptly, the chair protesting under my sudden movement, and walked toward the exit without another word.

[story 2]

I stared at the faded photograph on the mantelpiece, the one from the summer we swore we’d change the world. My knuckles were white where I gripped the frame. Liam was there, grinning, his arm slung casually around my shoulder. Now, the image felt like a deliberate insult. I couldn't look at him anymore; the sight of him felt like a physical assault. "You remember that?" I asked, my voice dangerously low, pitched just above a murmur, but laced with an undeniable edge. He looked up, startled, his expression shifting from mild confusion to something guarded. He frowned, a subtle tightening around his eyes that spoke volumes. "Who are you talking about?" he asked, his tone measured, almost distant. The air in the room seemed to thicken, suffocating. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to keep the tremor out of my hands. "Me," I stated, the single word cutting through the silence. He just blinked, a slow, unfocused movement, and then shrugged, a gesture so casual it felt like a deliberate dismissal of everything we’d ever been.

[story 3]

The silence stretched between us, taut and unforgiving. I felt a pressure building behind my eyes, a hot, stinging sensation that made my vision swim slightly. Every muscle in my body was coiled tight, ready to spring, yet I couldn't move. I watched Liam across the crowded room, his attention clearly focused elsewhere, completely oblivious to the seismic shift happening inside me. When he finally approached, his demeanor was one of simple courtesy, utterly lacking the familiarity that used to define our bond. "Hey," he said, his voice friendly, but it landed on me like a dull thud. I met his gaze, refusing to soften my expression, letting the raw feeling of being erased settle onto my features. I wanted to lash out, to demand an acknowledgment of the years we shared, but I held back, focusing instead on the precise, controlled rigidity of my posture. "It's strange," I said, my voice steady despite the tremor running through my hands. "Like I'm speaking to someone who doesn't know the story." I felt a burning resentment rise, hot and sharp, demanding release.
